title: Bruno Corbucci

infobox.value: (23 October 1931 – 7 September 1996) was an Italian [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter") and [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"). He was the younger brother of [Sergio Corbucci](/wiki/Sergio_Corbucci "Sergio Corbucci") and wrote many of his films. He was born in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), where he also died.

["Italian Comedy - The State of Things"](https://web.archive.org/web/20100801004911/http://www.labiennale.org/en/cinema/festival/lineup/off-sel/1937-1988/). *labiennale.org*. Archived from [the original](http://www.labiennale.org/en/cinema/festival/lineup/off-sel/1937-1988/) on 1 August 2010. Retrieved 1 August 2010.

Thrower, Stephen (2018) [May 1999]. *Beyond Terror: The Films of Lucio Fulci* (Revised & Expanded Second ed.). England, United Kingdom: Fab Press (published February 2018). p. 60. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-903254-90-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-903254-90-5 "Special:BookSources/978-1-903254-90-5").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Italian screenwriter and film director

| Bruno Corbucci | |
| --- | --- |
| Corbucci in 1960 | |
| Born | (1931-10-23)23 October 1931  [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") |
| Died | 7 September 1996(1996-09-07) (aged 64)  Rome, Italy |
| Occupations | [Screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter"), [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director") |

**Bruno Corbucci** (23 October 1931 – 7 September 1996) was an Italian [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter") and [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"). He was the younger brother of [Sergio Corbucci](/wiki/Sergio_Corbucci "Sergio Corbucci") and wrote many of his films. He was born in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), where he also died.

The vast majority of his directorial efforts are lowbrow comedies. He also directed *[I figli del leopardo](/wiki/I_figli_del_leopardo "I figli del leopardo")*, a parody of *[Il gattopardo](/wiki/The_Leopard_(1963_film) "The Leopard (1963 film)")*. His biggest success came with the long-running "Nico Giraldi" series, which starred [Tomas Milian](/wiki/Tomas_Milian "Tomas Milian") as a foul-mouthed Roman policeman.

His 1971 film *[Io non spezzo... rompo](/wiki/Io_non_spezzo..._rompo "Io non spezzo... rompo")* was shown as part of a retrospective on Italian comedy at the [67th Venice International Film Festival](/wiki/67th_Venice_International_Film_Festival "67th Venice International Film Festival").[[1]](#cite_note-venezia67-1)
